rows.v: The Dungeon Master — the player who runs the game, narrates the world, controls non-player characters, and adjudicates the rules. Part storyteller, part referee.
The DM described the torchlit dungeon and asked what the party wanted to do next.
From 'Dungeons & Dragons', the original game. Other systems call this role the Game Master (GM) or Storyteller, but the DM's job — running the world — is the same.
The polyhedral dice that drive the game's randomness. The twenty-sided 'd20' is iconic, but sets include d4, d6, d8, d10, and d12 too.
She scooped up her dice and rolled the d20, holding her breath as it tumbled.
'Dice' is ancient, but the polyhedral set is RPG signature. 'd20' means 'a die with 20 sides' — the 'd' notation (d6, d8) is universal tabletop shorthand.
Rolling dice to determine an outcome. A 'check' tests a skill against a target number — the core 'roll the dice and add your bonus' resolution of the game.
He had to roll a Stealth check to sneak past the sleeping guard.
From the everyday 'roll' of dice. The 'roll plus modifier versus a target' mechanic is the heartbeat of most tabletop RPGs — chance shaped by character ability.
Difficulty Class — the target number a player must meet or beat on a roll to succeed at a task. The DM sets the DC based on how hard the action is.
The DM set a DC of 15 for the tricky lock, and her roll just cleared it.
Descriptive: the 'class' (level) of 'difficulty'. A higher DC means a harder task — the simple dial a DM turns to make challenges easy or daunting.
'Advantage' lets you roll two dice and take the higher; 'disadvantage' makes you take the lower. An elegant way to reflect favorable or unfavorable circumstances.
Flanking the enemy, she attacked with advantage, rolling twice and keeping the better result.
A signature mechanic of D&D's fifth edition, replacing fiddly bonuses with a clean roll-twice system that intuitively models good and bad situations.
A number added to or subtracted from a dice roll, based on a character's abilities, skills, or circumstances. Your modifiers turn a raw roll into a result.
With a +5 modifier to Athletics, his climbing rolls were reliably strong.
From 'modify' — the bonus or penalty 'modifies' the raw die. Modifiers are how a character's training and talent tilt the odds in their favor (or against).
A 'natural 20' is rolling a 20 on the d20 before modifiers — often a critical success. A 'natural 1' is the opposite: an automatic, often comical failure.
She rolled a nat 20 on the impossible leap, and the whole table cheered.
'Natural' means the die's raw face value, unmodified. The nat 20 (triumph) and nat 1 (disaster) are tabletop's most dramatic, celebrated, and dreaded rolls.
An ongoing series of connected game sessions forming a continuous story. A campaign can span months or years as characters grow and the plot unfolds.
Their campaign had run for two years, the characters evolving from nobodies to legendary heroes.
From the military sense of a 'campaign' (a connected series of operations). It's the long-form story arc that distinguishes RPGs from one-off board games.
A character's profession or archetype — Fighter, Wizard, Rogue, Cleric — defining their abilities, role, and play style. The core of who your character is.
He chose the Wizard class, trading toughness for a spellbook full of devastating magic.
From 'class' (a category or type). A character's class determines their powers and role in the party — the fundamental build choice in most RPGs.
A character's species or ancestry — Human, Elf, Dwarf, Halfling — granting distinct traits and flavor. Many games now use 'species' or 'ancestry' instead.
She played a Dwarf, gaining darkvision and a sturdy resistance to poison.
From 'race' in the older sense of a kind or lineage. Modern games increasingly prefer 'species' or 'ancestry' to describe a character's heritage and innate traits.
A measure of a character's power and experience. Characters 'level up' by earning experience points, gaining new abilities and growing stronger over time.
After defeating the dragon, the party leveled up, unlocking powerful new abilities.
From 'level' (a rank or stage). Leveling up — the satisfying climb from weak novice to mighty hero — is one of the RPG's most rewarding loops.
The six core statistics defining a character: Strength, Dexterity, Constitution, Intelligence, Wisdom, and Charisma. They underpin nearly every roll.
He dumped his Charisma to boost Strength, building a mighty but socially hopeless warrior.
The numerical foundation of a character. These six scores (often rolled or assigned at creation) feed the modifiers behind almost every action in the game.
Hit Points — a measure of how much damage a character can take before falling unconscious or dying. HP drops as you're hurt and is restored by rest or healing.
Down to her last few hit points, she desperately quaffed a healing potion.
From the sense of how many 'hits' a character can take. HP is the universal health bar of RPGs — reach zero and your hero is in mortal trouble.
Armor Class — a number representing how hard a character is to hit. An attacker must roll equal to or above your AC to land a blow. Higher AC means tougher to strike.
The knight's heavy plate gave him an armor class so high most blows simply glanced off.
Descriptive: the 'class' (rating) of one's 'armor' and defenses. AC is the target number attackers must beat — the core of whether a hit connects.
A character's moral and ethical outlook, plotted on two axes (good–evil, lawful–chaotic). 'Chaotic Good' or 'Lawful Evil' sketch a character's guiding principles.
Her Chaotic Good rogue stole from tyrants but always shared the spoils with the poor.
A nine-box grid from early D&D crossing law-vs-chaos with good-vs-evil. A shorthand (and endless debate topic) for a character's moral compass.
A roll at the start of combat that determines turn order. Higher initiative acts first — crucial for getting your blows in before the enemy.
She rolled high on initiative and struck first, before the goblins could even react.
From 'initiative' (the right to begin). The initiative roll sets the sequence of a battle, deciding who seizes the first, often decisive, move.
On your turn, you typically get one main 'action' (attack, cast a spell) plus possibly a 'bonus action' for quicker moves. Managing your actions is core to combat tactics.
He used his action to attack and his bonus action to dash out of danger.
From 'action' (a thing done). The action economy — what you can do in a single turn — is the tactical puzzle at the heart of RPG combat.
A limited resource representing a spellcaster's daily magical energy. Casting a spell expends a slot of the appropriate level, recovered after a rest.
Down to her last spell slot, the wizard saved it for an emergency.
Descriptive: a 'slot' that holds a spell's energy. The system limits how much magic a caster can unleash before resting, forcing careful resource management.
A minor spell that can be cast at will, without using a spell slot. Cantrips are a caster's reliable, unlimited basic magic.
Out of spell slots, the wizard fell back on his trusty Fire Bolt cantrip.
An old word for a magic trick or charm (from Scots). In RPGs, cantrips are the simplest spells — weak but endlessly repeatable, a caster's basic attack.
A roll to resist or avoid a harmful effect — dodging a fireball, shrugging off poison, resisting a charm. Your last line of defense against danger.
He made his saving throw against the dragon's breath, taking only half the damage.
From 'save' (to avoid harm). The saving throw is the defender's roll — a chance to 'save' yourself from spells, traps, and effects that would otherwise hit full force.
Area of Effect — a spell or attack that strikes everything in a region, like a fireball engulfing a room. AoE rewards catching enemies bunched together.
The wizard dropped a fireball, an area of effect that caught the whole cluster of orcs.
Borrowed from broader gaming. An 'area of effect' spell hits a zone rather than one target — devastating against grouped foes, risky near allies.
A Critical Hit — rolling a natural 20 on an attack, dealing extra damage (often double dice). The thrilling jackpot of combat rolls.
Her natural 20 scored a crit, and the doubled damage felled the boss in one blow.
Short for 'critical hit'. Triggered by a natural 20, the crit is combat's big payoff — a moment of doubled damage and table-wide excitement.
The group of player characters adventuring together. The party is the team at the heart of the game, ideally balancing different classes and roles.
The party of four — a fighter, wizard, cleric, and rogue — set out into the dungeon together.
From 'party' (a group with a shared purpose, as in a 'search party'). The adventuring party is the cooperative core of the tabletop experience.
Non-Player Character — anyone in the game world controlled by the DM rather than a player: shopkeepers, villains, allies, and quest-givers.
The mysterious NPC in the tavern offered them a map in exchange for a dangerous favor.
An acronym for 'non-player character', borrowed across all of gaming. NPCs populate the world the players move through, voiced and run by the DM.
A preliminary meeting before a campaign begins, where the group sets expectations, creates characters, and agrees on tone, rules, and boundaries.
At session zero, they agreed on the campaign's tone and drew up everyone's backstories together.
Named as the 'zeroth' session before play begins. A modern best practice for aligning the group's expectations and avoiding conflicts down the road.
A discrete challenge the party faces — a fight, a puzzle, a negotiation, a trap. Encounters are the building blocks of an adventure's action.
The next encounter was a tense standoff with bandits that could turn to violence or talk.
From 'encounter' (a meeting, often hostile). Each distinct challenge — combat or otherwise — is an 'encounter', the unit DMs use to pace an adventure.
Custom content created by the DM or players rather than from official rulebooks — homemade monsters, items, rules, or entire worlds.
The DM ran a homebrew world of her own design, full of custom monsters and lore.
From home-brewing (making your own beer). It captures the do-it-yourself spirit of crafting your own game material instead of relying on published books.
When a DM forces the party down a predetermined path, removing meaningful choice. A common criticism of overly controlling game-running.
The players felt railroaded when every alternative they tried was mysteriously blocked.
From a train 'railroaded' onto fixed tracks. It describes a DM funneling players along a rigid plot, denying them the agency that makes RPGs special.
The treasure, gear, gold, and magic items the party earns from adventures. Distributing loot fairly is a perennial source of both joy and table drama.
The dragon's hoard yielded enough loot to outfit the entire party in magic gear.
From 'loot' (plunder). The reward for victory — gold, weapons, and magic items — and the cause of countless good-natured (and not-so-good-natured) party arguments.
Optimizing a character to maximize strengths and minimize weaknesses, squeezing out peak mechanical power — sometimes at the expense of roleplay or balance.
His min-maxed build hit like a truck but couldn't hold a conversation to save his life.
A compound of 'minimize' and 'maximize'. The min-maxer dumps unimportant stats to push key ones to the limit, chasing raw effectiveness over rounded character.
Slang for a player whose character solves every problem with violence and has no home, ties, or restraint — wandering the land killing and looting.
The party devolved into murder hobos, attacking every NPC instead of talking to them.
A vivid compound: a homeless ('hobo') wanderer who murders their way through the world. A gently mocking term for a thoughtlessly violent play style.
Total Party Kill — when every player character dies in a single encounter. The catastrophic, campaign-ending (or restarting) wipeout every party dreads.
A single misjudged fight against the dragon ended in a brutal TPK.
An acronym for 'total party kill'. The ultimate disaster — the whole party wiped out at once — feared, mourned, and sometimes laughed about for years after.
Using out-of-character knowledge (player knowledge) to make in-character decisions your character wouldn't actually know. Generally frowned upon as breaking immersion.
It was metagaming for her to avoid the trap her character had no way of knowing about.
From 'meta' (above/beyond the game). Acting on knowledge from outside the fiction — that a monster is weak to fire, say — when the character couldn't know it.
A player who frequently invokes and argues the precise rules, often to their advantage. Helpful when the group's stuck, exhausting when it derails play.
The rules lawyer paused the game for ten minutes to debate the exact wording of the spell.
A compound likening the player to a 'lawyer' arguing the letter of the 'rules'. Their precision can clarify or, when overdone, grind the fun to a halt.
A Critical Failure — rolling a natural 1, often causing an automatic, sometimes catastrophically funny failure. The dreaded flip side of the crit.
His nat 1 crit-fail sent the arrow flying backward into his own ally.
Short for 'critical failure'. The natural 1's comic disaster — many tables add house rules turning it into spectacular, memorable mishaps.
